content:
5 min read 3 hours ago -- A web developer’s honest, jargon-free guide to starting React Native (with Expo). A few weeks ago I was a web developer. I knew React, I knew how to build pages, and I was comfortable. Then one day the task changed: “We need this as a mobile app now.” And just like that, I was standing in front of a world I had only used as a customer, never built for. If you’re reading this, maybe you’re in the same spot. You know a little React, or maybe you’re just curious about how the apps on your phone are actually made. Either way, let me walk you through it the way I wish someone had walked me through it — slowly, and without scary words. How Were Mobile Apps Built Before All This? For a long time, building a mobile app meant picking a side: • Android app → written in Kotlin (or Java before that) • iPhone app → written in Swift (or Objective-C before that) These are still very real and very powerful -a huge number of apps on your phone right now are written in exactly these languages. But here’s the catch that made life hard: • Android and iOS are two completely different worlds. • If your company wanted an app on both, you usually needed two teams, two codebases, and two sets of bugs. • You’d build a screen once in Kotlin for Android, then build the same screen again in Swift for iOS. • Same app, double the work. For a small team or a solo developer, that’s a lot. Then Came React Native React Native answered a simple question: What if you could write your app once, in one language, and run it on both Android and iOS? Key points: • That language is JavaScript (or TypeScript -JavaScript with a safety belt). • You write your screens once, and React Native runs them on both platforms. • It does not translate your code into another language -it runs your JavaScript and uses it to drive the phone’s real native building blocks. • When you write a box, React Native reaches out and controls an actual native view underneath. • So it’s not a website pretending to be an app -it’s an actual app that gets installed from the store. And the best part for someone like me? It’s built on React. Same components, same way of thinking, same hooks. If you’ve ever written a React component for the web, you already know more React Native than you think. Why It Felt Like Home (and Where It Felt Strange) When I opened my first React Native screen, a lot of it looked familiar: • You still write components. • You still use useState and useEffect. • You still pass props. That part felt like coming home. But then the small differences showed up -and they’re worth knowing early. Quick Comparison: Web vs. React Native •
